stats.headline: Part 2 of two: **three grammar rule families** tested in almost every PSLE English Booklet A — verb forms, connectors & tenses, and idiomatic prepositions.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome to Grammar MCQ — Part 2
keyConcepts.body: We'll cover the **next three grammar rule families** that show up in almost every PSLE English Booklet A. After each rule, you get an interactive question to try — pick an answer, hit Submit, and see if you're right.
bullets: **4. Verb forms** — gerund vs infinitive, causative bare infinitive
**5. Connectors & tenses** — **since / although / in spite of** + perfect tenses
**6. Idiomatic prepositions** — **congratulate on**, **robbed of**, **under the impression**
keyConcepts.callout: Recognise the rule type from the options, then apply.

keyConcepts.title: 4: Verb forms — gerund vs infinitive vs bare
keyConcepts.body: The form of the SECOND verb in a sentence depends on what comes before it. Three main patterns to know.
bullets:
**Verbs followed by GERUND (-ing):**
   enjoy, avoid, mind, consider, suggest, finish, dislike, **have trouble**, **have difficulty**, object to, look forward to, be used to
   → "Bella enjoys **interacting** with her neighbours."

**Verbs followed by INFINITIVE (to + base):**
   want, hope, decide, agree, plan, learn, refuse, manage, expect, seem
   → "Mohan seems **to have lost** weight."

**The PREPOSITION trap.** When "to" is a preposition (NOT the infinitive **to**), the next verb is a **gerund**:
   • *object **to** going*, *look forward **to** seeing*
   • *be used **to** working*, *be accustomed **to** doing*
   ⚠️ Students see "to" and pick the base form by reflex — that's wrong here.

**Causative / sensory verbs + BARE infinitive (no "to"):**
   let, make, help, watch, see, hear, witness, feel, notice
   → "Kamal **made** his baby sister **giggle**."
   → "Judy **witnessed** a boy **slip** the cards into his bag."

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Verb forms

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2021 Q7 — the "to + gerund" preposition trap
interactiveQuiz.stem: Tom **objected to** ___ on a camping trip as he did not like outdoor activities.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "object **to**" LOOKS like the infinitive "to go" — but "to" here is a PREPOSITION. Test: try a noun → "objected to **the trip**" ✓ → preposition confirmed. Preposition + verb → **GERUND** (-ing form) → **going**.

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2022 Q5 — causative bare infinitive
interactiveQuiz.stem: Judy **witnessed** a boy ___ the game cards into his bag, so she reported it to the store manager.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **"witness"** is a SENSORY verb (like **see, watch, hear, notice**) — these take the **bare infinitive** (no "to", no "-ing", no past tense). → **slip**.

keyConcepts.callout: **Two rules:** "to + verb" can be EITHER an infinitive OR a preposition — test by trying a noun in its place. **Causative / sensory verbs** (let, make, help, see, watch, hear, witness, feel, notice) take the **bare base form** of the next verb.

keyConcepts.title: 5: Connectors — cause / concession / time
keyConcepts.body: Each connector signals a specific logical relationship. Read the SECOND clause to figure out which one fits.
bullets: **Cause:** **since, because, as, owing to, due to, because of, in response to**. → "**Since** the concert was cancelled, the organiser refunded us."
**Concession (contradiction):** **although, even though, despite, in spite of**. The two clauses contradict. → "**In spite of** the continual support, the family is unable to make ends meet."
**Contrast:** **other than, on the other hand, whereas, but**. → "Li Shan does not eat seafood. **Other than** that, she will eat anything."
**Time:** **as soon as, when, while, before, after, until, by the time**. These often dictate the TENSE of the next clause.
**"In spite of / Despite"** must be followed by a NOUN or GERUND, not a clause. "In spite of **being late**" ✓, "In spite of **she was late**" ✗ (use "although" instead).

keyConcepts.title: 5: Tense triggers — words that force a tense
keyConcepts.body: Some grammar questions test **tense triggers** — words that force a specific tense in the next clause.
bullets: **"Had + subject + past participle"** = a formal way of saying "if X had…". → "**Had James put** the compass in his bag, we would not be lost" = "If James **had put** the compass in his bag…"
**"wish / if only" + past perfect** for regret. → "Muthu **wished** he **had heeded** his mother's advice."
**"since / for the past N"** + present perfect (continuous). → "The news **has been circulating** since yesterday."
**"by the end of [future time]"** + future perfect. → "By next year, my brother **will have become** a trained instructor."

**"No sooner had X done than Y…"** — a fancy way of saying "as soon as X happened, Y happened". The first half starts with **"had"** in front of the person, then a **"done"** verb (had arrived, had eaten); the second half is normal past tense. → "**No sooner had** I **arrived** home **than** it started to rain."

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Connectors & tenses

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2022 Q6 — concession connector
interactiveQuiz.stem: ___ the continual support from the villagers, the family is unable to make ends meet.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Clause 1 "continual support" is HELPFUL. Clause 2 "unable to make ends meet" is the OPPOSITE result. Contradiction → CONCESSION connector → **In spite of**. **Owing to** signals cause (wrong direction). **Amid** / **With regard to** don't match the meaning.

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2024 Q6 — conditional inversion (flipped "if")
interactiveQuiz.stem: '___ James put the compass in his bag, we would not be lost in the forest,' grumbled Ai Ling.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: The consequence "**would not be lost**" tells us the first clause must be **past perfect**. Start from "If James **had put**…" then drop **if** and flip the order → "**Had** James put…". Only **Had** triggers this flip. **Did** gives past simple; **Might/Should** don't form a valid conditional with the given consequence.

keyConcepts.callout: **For connectors**, read the SECOND clause and ask "does it support or contradict the first?" — that picks cause vs concession. **For tense triggers**, the consequence ("would have / would be") tells you the first clause needs past perfect → **Had X done**.

keyConcepts.title: 6: Idiomatic prepositions — fixed combinations
keyConcepts.body: There's no rule — these are FIXED idioms you have to memorise. PSLE picks pairs where multiple options sound vaguely OK.
bullets:
**Verb + preposition pairs** (commit these as a single chunk):
   • congratulate someone **on** something
   • accuse someone **of** something
   • blame X **for** Y
   • rob someone **of** something
   • suspect someone **of** something
   • depend / rely **on** someone
   • succeed **in** doing

**Idiomatic phrases with a fixed preposition:**
   • **under** the impression
   • **in** someone's face ("shut the door in her face")
   • **on** purpose, **on** time, **on** behalf of
   • **at** risk, **at** a loss, **at** stake
   • **by** accident, **by** chance, **by** mistake
   • **against** the current, **against** the rules
   • **out of** breath, **out of** stock
**Movement vs position:** **into** (movement) vs **in** (position). "He walked **into** the room" vs "He is **in** the room". Same for **onto / on**, **off of / off**.
**"X minutes into the game"** — "into" with a duration marks a point in time.

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Idiomatic prepositions

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2024 Q10 — verb + preposition fixed pair
interactiveQuiz.stem: The police officers were looking for the man who **robbed the tourists** ___ their possessions.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: The verb **"rob"** has a fixed idiom: **"rob someone OF something"**. **for** is used with **blame** ("blame him for it"), **off** is informal/wrong here, **with** is for weapons ("robbed him with a knife"). → **of**.

interactiveQuiz.label: PSLE 2021 Q9 — idiomatic phrase preposition
interactiveQuiz.stem: As the guard was ___ the impression that the hall was empty, he locked it up, not realising that someone was still in it.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "___ the impression" is a fixed phrase — the correct preposition is **"under the impression that…"**. **in**, **on**, **with** don't combine with "impression" in this idiom.

keyConcepts.callout: **Memorise as chunks.** **rob someone OF, accuse someone OF, congratulate someone ON, blame someone FOR, under the impression, in someone's face, against the current, by accident.** Don't reason them out — they're fixed by usage.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put the three Part 2 rules together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style Grammar MCQ questions across all 3 sub-topics — with PSLE source questions prioritised first.
bullets: **15 questions** — **5 questions per sub-topic**.
PSLE questions come first, then top-up from school exam papers.
After you submit, the AI flags which rule families you got wrong and suggests a focused follow-up quiz.
**Mistakes are saved** — we'll return to them in an auto-revision session in **1 week**.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: The -ing form of a verb used as a noun (running, swimming). Comes after prepositions and certain verbs (enjoy, avoid).
The 'to + base' form (to go, to see). Used after verbs like want, hope, decide, plan.
The base form WITHOUT 'to' (go, see). Used after causative verbs (let, make, help, watch).
Formal way of writing 'if X had…' — flip the auxiliary to the front: 'Had X done…'
Connects a dependent clause to a main clause: since, although, when, unless, while.

subTopics.label: Verb forms
subTopics.description: Gerund vs infinitive vs bare infinitive after a verb. Includes the 'to + gerund' preposition trap (object to going, look forward to seeing) and causative bare infinitive (made her giggle, witnessed him slip).

subTopics.label: Connectors & tense markers
subTopics.description: Cause / concession / contrast / time connectors (since, although, in spite of, when) plus tense triggers — conditional inversion ('Had X done…'), wish + past perfect, since + present perfect continuous.

subTopics.label: Idiomatic prepositions
subTopics.description: Fixed verb + preposition combinations (congratulate ON, rob OF, accuse OF, blame FOR) and idiomatic phrases (under the impression, in someone's face, against the current, by accident).
